texts.label: text
texts.orig: Exploring the lithium resources in concentrated seawater/salt lake brine could provide necessary support for the sustainable development in future. Selective-electrodialysis (S-ED, equipped with monovalent selective ion-exchange membranes) is considered as an effective way to reduce the ratio of magnesium to lithium in concentrated seawater/salt lake brines. However, the effectiveness of the prefractionation of lithium chloride from brine is not clearly investigated, because both seawater and salt lake brines are complex mixtures. Nowadays, only simple systems with binary or ternary cations system have been investigated. Based on a clean production process for the utilization of concentrated seawater/salt lake brine, the prefractionation of LiCl from concentrated seawater/salt lake brines by S-ED was investigated in this work. From the concentrated seawater experiments, it is bene fi cial to improve the RLi (recovery ratio of Li þ ) at a higher voltage, but an excessively high working voltage is adverse to the preliminary separation between Li þ and Mg 2 þ ; a bigger VC/VD (initial volume ratio of concentrating and desalting solution, VD ¼ 2.5 L in this paper) is favorable to increase RLi and reduce ESEC (speci fi c energy consumption of Li þ ). At the optimal voltage of 7 V and VC/VD of 0.6, the mole ratio of LiCl: MgCl2: MgSO4 increased from 1: 2.227: 2.463 to 1: 1.461: 0.085. For the salt lake brines, the optimal voltage for the LiCl prefractionation in the selected brine system was 10 V, which had a higher RLi of 76.45% and an appropriate ESEC of 0.66 kWh/(mol Li). Finally, the parameters of RLi, ESEC and separation effect of LiCl were discussed for the salt lakes of West Taijinar, East Taijinar and Yiliping in China. It is found that the East Taijinar salt lake brine was more suitable to obtain LiCl at a lower ESEC.

texts.orig: (Whittingham, 2004; Tsuyoshi, 2015). Currently, lithium resource is mainly obtained from lithium ore or salt lake brine (Choubey et al., 2016). The main methods for extracting lithium from ores include pyro-metallurgy, pressure leaching and bioleaching (Basudev, 2017). Because of the feature of low and non-renewable reserve and unbalanced distribution in the lithium ore, the energy consumption of extraction lithium from mineral is high, and the process is not an environmentally friendly one. Therefore, lithium is mainly extracted from salt lakes and subsurface brines now (Gruber et al., 2011). The methods of precipitation (Chen et al., 2017), liquidliquid extraction (Hamzaoui et al., 2003), sorption (Chitrakar et al., 2014; Xiao et al., 2012), ion-exchange (Wang et al., 2006), nanofi ltration (NF) (Somrani et al., 2013; Wen et al., 2006) and electrodialysis (ED) (Liu et al., 2015) were mainly used to extract lithium

texts.orig: from solutions. In addition, a new electrochemical method (Pasta et al., 2012; Lee et al., 2013; Zhao et al., 2017) was developed to extract lithium. Due to its high selectivity, low energy consumption and limited exchange capacity, the electrochemical method is potentially suitable for recovering lithium from solutions with lower lithium ion concentration. The traditional methods of lithium recovery have the disadvantages of high energy consumption, low utilization of resources and high pollution (Basudev, 2017). And with the popularization of cleaner production idea, a method of lithium recovery from salt lake brines with low energy consumption and pollution is urgently needed to be developed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The salt lake brine is the main resource (accounting for 59% of total reserves on land) for lithium reserve (Basudev, 2017). In general, if the mass ratio of Mg 2 þ /Li þ is less than eight, it is considered as a low Mg 2 þ /Li þ ratio brine (Yu et al., 2013). The traditional methods are effective for recovering lithium from low Mg 2 þ /Li þ ratio brine. However, the mass ratios of Mg 2 þ /Li þ for most of salt lakes in the world are higher than 8. It is dif fi cult and uneconomical to recover lithium from high Mg 2 þ /Li þ ratio brine by traditional method because of the similar chemical properties between magnesium and lithium. In addition, the reserve capacity of lithium resource in seawater is about 15,000 times than that on land (Tsuyoshi, 2013). With the rapid development of seawater desalination industry, the direct discharge of concentrated seawater to the ocean would cause the destruction of marine ecological environment in the end (Belatoui et al., 2017; Belkin et al., 2015). The utilization of concentrated seawater and a Zero Discharge Desalination (ZDD) based on seawater desalination have been proposed (Liu et al., 2016; Guo et al., 2016). The concentration of lithium in the concentrated seawater is about double of that in the seawater. Therefore, lithium in the concentrated seawater will become an important source for lithium extraction in the future. However, it is inef fi cient to extract lithium from concentrated seawater by traditional method due to its lower lithium concentration (about 0.34 mg/L) and high Mg 2 þ /Li þ ratio (about 7.4 -10 6 ). Hence, the preliminary separation and concentration of Li þ are essential.

texts.orig: As an electro-driving membrane separation process, ED has been widely used in seawater desalination, salt production (Liu et al., 2016; Tanaka et al., 2003; Zhang et al., 2017), wastewater treatment (Mohammadi et al., 2004; Lu et al., 2015; Pisarska et al., 2017; Scarazzato et al., 2017) and other processes (Strathmann, 2010). The ED process can be classi fi ed as conventional electrodialysis (C-ED, often shortened to ED) and selective electrodialysis (SED). The C-ED stack is equipped with general ion exchange membranes, and monovalent selective ion exchange membranes are equipped in the S-ED stack. Due to the strong retention of multivalent ions and high permeability of monovalent ions of the membrane, S-ED can be used to separate monovalent and multivalent ions. Compared to the NF, S-ED has been applied in wider fi elds due to its lower operating pressure, higher monovalent ionic permeability, better concentrating effect and less requirement for pretreatment (Lambert et al., 2006; Walha et al., 2007). It was found that the ionic permeability in the membranes is reverse to its radius of hydrated ion and hydration free energy (Berrin, 2012; Mukherjee and Sengupta, 2003; Chen et al., 2018). Some researches on the separation of Li þ from high Mg 2 þ /Li þ brines have been reported (Nie et al., 2017). The separation of magnesium and lithium also has been well achieved in our previous work (Ji et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2018). However, the feed compositions in the above studies are not consistent with real concentrated seawater or natural brine, because only simple systems with binary or ternary cations system are investigated. In addition, the effect of coexisting ions in real solution on lithium ion transport has not been clearly discussed. In the process of lithium extraction by industrial electrodialysis in different systems, the suitable operating voltage and other conditions will change and the concentration diffusion cannot be ignored under the condition of high ionic concentration. Therefore, it is necessary to explore the in fl uences of coexisting ions and volume ratio of concentrating and desalting solution on the migration of lithium ions to fi nd appropriate operating conditions in the process of extracting lithium chloride from concentrated seawater and natural brine. In this work, as shown in Fig.1, a complete process for utilization of concentrated seawater/salt lake brine based on two stages S-ED has been proposed. The fi rst stage of the S-ED process is used to preliminarily separate lithium chloride; the concentrated solution from the fi rst stage S-ED process, which enriched LiCl, NaCl and KCl, can be used to obtain Li2CO3 by addition of Na2CO3 (Basudev, 2017; Wang et al., 2018) (The by-product of the reaction is NaCl and no other impurity is introduced for downstream processes), and the remaining mother liquor can be used to produce sodium chloride and potassium chloride. The desalted solution, which enriches MgCl2, MgSO4 and a small amount of NaCl and KCl, can be used for second stage of the S-ED process to recycle the remaining NaCl and KCl. The desalted solution from the second stage S-ED process can be used to obtain MgCl2 and MgSO4. Due to lower concentration of interfering ions, the purity of the products obtained in the process will be high. This craft can meet with the need of cleaner production to some extent (Scarazzato et al., 2017). The aim of this work shown in the dotted rectangle is to investigate

texts.orig: The schematic diagram of S-ED process, which is composed of direct current (DC) system, transmission channel and membrane stack, is shown in Fig. 2. The membrane stack was equipped with 11 monovalent selective cation exchange membranes (CIMS) and 10 monovalent selective anion exchange membranes (ACS). The additional CIMS was installed next to the anode to prevent Cl � from entering the anolyte stream and making chlorine gas then damaging the membranes. Some parameters of the membranes can refer to the previous literature (Ji et al., 2017). The size of the membranes, effective membrane size of each pair and the spacer thickness were shown in Fig. 2. The transmission channel was consisted of three pumps provided by Shanghai Xinxishan Industrial Ltd. China, and the pumps were supplied to the circulation system of concentrating chamber, desalination chamber and electrolyte chamber. Cathode compartment shared the same electrolyte solution tank and the same pump with anode compartment. This arrangement reduced the loss of the lithium ion, because the lithium ion moving out of the desalination chamber would return to the concentrating chamber. The DC system (JS3020D) was produced by Wuxi ANS Electronic Technology Ltd. China. Na2SO4 solution (5 wt%) was used as the electrode solution. In order to reduce the effect of concentration diffusion from desalting compartment to concentrating compartment in earlier stage of the experiments, NaCl solution (0.5 mol/L) was added to the concentrating compartment as recovery solution. The LiCl solution can also be used as the recovery solution for lithium chloride, but the NaCl solution was selected in order to reduce measurement error of Li þ . In this study, the linear fl ow velocities in desalting/concentrating/ electrolyte cells were set to 6.2/6.2/3.8 cm/s. The migration of ions in the membrane stack is shown in Fig. 3. Under the direct current, Li þ and Cl � permeate through the monovalent cation exchange membrane and anion exchange membrane to the concentrating compartment, respectively. While most Ca 2 þ , Mg 2 þ and SO4 2 � are trapped in the desalting solution. By this approach, lithium chloride can be pre-separated from concentrated seawater/salt lake brines.

texts.orig: After considering the occurrence of concentration polarization and the in fl uence of the limiting current, the experiments at different applied voltages with the range from 3 to 9 V, were carried out to explore the effects of applied voltages on the preliminary separation of LiCl from concentrated seawater. In this part, the initial volume ratio (VC/VD) is 0.6. The variation of the concentrations of Li þ , Mg 2 þ and Ca 2 þ in concentrating compartment at different voltages is shown in Fig. 4. All cationic concentrations rise in different extents with time. It also indicates that the concentrations of Li þ , Mg 2 þ and Ca 2 þ increase with the rising applied voltages. This is because that the mass transfer driving force of lithium ion increases and the leakage of Mg 2 þ and Ca 2 þ becomes worse with the rising voltage. Furthermore, under lower voltages of 3 V and 5 V, the rising velocity of Li þ concentration increases with time. This might be due to the smaller hydrated ionic radius of Na þ and K þ (Berrin, 2012), a strong competitive effect on the migration of Li þ at the beginning of the experiment; with the migration of Na þ and K þ , this competition will be weakened and make the rising velocity of the Li þ concentration increasing. When the applied voltage is 7 V, the concentration of Li þ in the concentrating cell rises linearly with time. However, when the voltage increases to 9 V, the driving force is greater and more lithium ions are migrated at the beginning. texts.text: After considering the occurrence of concentration polarization and the in fl uence of the limiting current, the experiments at different applied voltages with the range from 3 to 9 V, were carried out to explore the effects of applied voltages on the preliminary separation of LiCl from concentrated seawater. In this part, the initial volume ratio (VC/VD) is 0.6. The variation of the concentrations of Li þ , Mg 2 þ and Ca 2 þ in concentrating compartment at different voltages is shown in Fig. 4. All cationic concentrations rise in different extents with time. It also indicates that the concentrations of Li þ , Mg 2 þ and Ca 2 þ increase with the rising applied voltages. This is because that the mass transfer driving force of lithium ion increases and the leakage of Mg 2 þ and Ca 2 þ becomes worse with the rising voltage. Furthermore, under lower voltages of 3 V and 5 V, the rising velocity of Li þ concentration increases with time. This might be due to the smaller hydrated ionic radius of Na þ and K þ (Berrin, 2012), a strong competitive effect on the migration of Li þ at the beginning of the experiment; with the migration of Na þ and K þ , this competition will be weakened and make the rising velocity of the Li þ concentration increasing. texts.orig: Fig. 7 shows the variations of current ef fi ciency and ESEC with time at different applied voltages. The value of current ef fi ciency re fl ects the competition level between coexisting ions and lithium ion. The greater the current ef fi ciency is, the fewer other ions occupy the current, and the weaker the competition effect of coexisting cations on Li þ is. It can be seen a certain trend from Fig. 7(a) that the current ef fi ciency decreases fi rstly and then increases with the rising of voltage. The ESEC curves shown in Fig. 7(b) present an opposite trend. It mainly attributes to the lower voltage which would induce less ions migrate to the membrane interface and result in weaker resistance and impact on the migration of Li þ . The resistance would be enhanced with the rising voltage, and more multivalent ions migrate to the membrane interface, then results in lower current ef fi ciency and higher ESEC. However, the increasing rate of the driving force would excess the resistance of Li þ at higher voltages, and then cause higher current ef fi ciency and lower ESEC.

texts.orig: 3.1.2. Effect of initial volume ratio of concentrating and desalting solution (VC/VD)
texts.text: 3.1.2. Effect of initial volume ratio of concentrating and desalting solution (VC/VD)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The initial volume ratio of concentrating and desalting solution (VC/VD) is an important in fl uence factor in batch S-ED process. At the same applied voltage, the smaller the VC/VD value, the faster the increasing rate of ionic concentration. And the in fl uence of concentration diffusion on lithium separation is greater at lower VC/VD value. Therefore, different initial volumes of concentrated solutions (1 e 2 L, i.e. VC/VD values of 0.4 e 0.8) under the same initial volume of desalting solution (2.5 L) and voltage (7 V) were used to explore the effect of VC/VD on the fractionation performance.
texts.text: The initial volume ratio of concentrating and desalting solution (VC/VD) is an important in fl uence factor in batch S-ED process. At the same applied voltage, the smaller the VC/VD value, the faster the increasing rate of ionic concentration. And the in fl uence of concentration diffusion on lithium separation is greater at lower VC/VD value. Therefore, different initial volumes of concentrated solutions (1 e 2 L, i.e. VC/VD values of 0.4 e 0.8) under the same initial volume of desalting solution (2.5 L) and voltage (7 V) were used to explore the effect of VC/VD on the fractionation performance.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The changes of Li þ , Mg 2 þ and Ca 2 þ concentrations with time in different VC/VD values are shown in Fig. 9. It can be seen that with the decreasing of VC/VD, the ionic concentration in the concentrated solution is greatly increased. However, this is not due to the increasing of ionic fl ux, but the smaller volume of the concentrated solution. In order to further illustrate the in fl uence of VC/VD, a concept of ionic fl ux was introduced to do deep analysis in this part exposition. It can be seen from Fig. 10 that the ionic fl ux (For a consistency of time displayed in the paper, the each average fl ux value from time zero to time t in this section is represented by the fl ux at time t.) increases with the rising of VC/VD value overall. This is because a higher Li þ concentration with a lower VC/VD, as shown in Fig. 9, results in a more negative effect of concentration diffusion on the migration of Li þ . In addition, the fl uxes of Li þ and Ca 2 þ show the trend of increasing fi rst and then decreasing, and the fl ux of Mg 2 þ shows an opposite trend. This phenomenon can be explained by the process of coexisting ions transport through the membranes. Foremost, Mg 2 þ can reach the membrane interface fi rst due to its high charge and high concentration, so the fl ux of Mg 2 þ is biggest at the beginning of the experiment. And then, when the low-content ions of Ca 2 þ and Li þ reach the membrane interface, the fl ux of Ca 2 þ and Li þ increases due to their small-hydrated ionic radius, which causes the fl ux of Mg 2 þ decreased. Finally, with the decrease of Li þ and Ca 2 þ concentrations in desalting solution, the Mg 2 þ with high original concentration will be used to carry the current again and its concentration also increases with time. The phenomenon also proves that the selectivity of the membrane to calcium is better than that of magnesium, which is consistent with our previous study that the in fl uence order of coexisting cations on lithium migration is contrary to their hydrated radius sequence: K þ > Na þ > Ca 2 þ > Mg 2 þ (Chen et al., 2018).

texts.orig: Generally, a suitable operating voltage in S-ED process is intimately associated with the ionic strength of feed solution. So, the optimum operating voltages for purifying LiCl from various salt lake brines, which have a stronger ionic strength compared to the concentrated seawater, should be different from the concentrated seawater. In the following experiments, the brine from East Taijinar salt lake was selected fi rstly to investigate the effects of coexisting ions on the puri fi cation of LiCl at different applied voltages (6, 8, 10 and 12 V), which is based on the S-ED setup running below the limit current. The operating time was increased to 180 min for each group of experiments due to higher ionic concentration of this brine. texts.orig: Due to the different content of the ions in the actual salt lake brine system, M n þ /Li þ (Na þ /Li þ , K þ /Li þ , Mg 2 þ /Li þ , Ca 2 þ /Li þ ) and Cl � /SO4 2 � change as well. In this section, the comparison of the puri fi cation of LiCl based on different high Mg 2 þ /Li þ salt lake brines was investigated at 10 V. The changes in the concentrations of Li þ , Mg 2 þ and Ca 2 þ for the three salt lake brines with time are shown in Fig. 20. Higher lithium content and the resulting lower M n þ /Li þ (as shown in Table 2) induce the concentration of Li þ in concentrated solution of East Taijinar salt lake brine increasing signi fi cantly compared with other brines. It means the in fl uence of coexisting ions in the East Taijinar brine on the migration of Li þ is smaller than that of other two salt lakes. At the same time, the concentration of Li þ and the ratio of K þ /Li þ in Yiliping are very close to that of West Taijinar. However, the Na þ /Li þ ratio in Yiliping is 250.31, which is smaller than that of West Taijinar (322.63). Therefore, it leads to a weaker competition between Na þ and Li þ and results in a rapid increasing of Li þ for Yiliping compared with West Taijinar salt lake.

For the concentrated seawater, RLi could be signi fi cantly improved and the current ef fi ciency would decrease fi rst and then increase with the voltage value rising. 7 V was considered as the suitable voltage in the lithium chloride recovery process for the concentrated seawater. Higher VC/VD would result in higher RLi, higher current ef fi ciency and lower ESEC due to the smaller effect of concentration diffusion. However, considering the concentrating effect and ESEC together, the optimal VC/VD value was 0.6 for the concentrated seawater. Under the optimal conditions, the mole ratio of LiCl: MgCl2: MgSO4 changed to 1: 1.461: 0.085 at 120 min in the S-ED process.

texts.orig: The optimal voltage for recovering LiCl from salt lake brines (East Taijinar) was 10 V. At this voltage, the RLi was 76.45% and ESEC was 0.66 kWh/(mol Li). For the three salt lake brines investigated, the order of LiCl extraction ef fi ciency was East Taijinar > Yiliping > West Taijinar. The reason might be the competition of the coexisting ions in the brines. Although the initial ratios of Mg/Li are high, LiCl can be obtained preliminarily and effectively with the SED process.
